food_name: Kuih Bingka Ubi (Baked Tapioca Coconut Cake)
aliases: Kuih Bingka Ubi (Baked Tapioca Coconut Cake)
烤木薯糕 (kǎo mù shǔ gāo)

chinese: 烤木薯糕 (kǎo mù shǔ gāo)

food_subgroup: Traditional baked kuih — grated cassava with thick coconut milk and palm sugar

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn URI exists for kuih bingka ubi. Closest parent concept: baked cassava-based traditional Southeast Asian confection. FoodOn does not yet enumerate Southeast Asian traditional kuih at this specificity.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No exact FoodEx2 code for kuih bingka ubi. Closest parent category: baked products. Code A0010000 is a broad category for baked goods.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code for this traditional Southeast Asian food item.

main_ingredients: palm sugar (gula melaka) or brown sugar

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard piece of kuih bingka ubi weighs approximately 100g and represents a typical single-serve portion as sold at traditional Malaysian and Singaporean wet markets and kuih stalls.

nutrient_source.title: Singapore Health Promotion Board (HPB) Foodo Nutrient Database — Kuih / Traditional Cakes; supplemented by IMR Malaysia MyFCD cassava and coconut milk component data
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) and Institute for Medical Research Malaysia (IMR)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Primary macronutrient and micronutrient values sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo database for traditional baked kuih and cross-referenced with IMR Malaysia MyFCD cassava (raw, grated) and thick coconut milk component entries. Trace mineral values (copper, manganese, vitamin K) estimated from USDA FoodData Central cassava and coconut milk entries where HPB/MyFCD data were not available. All values represent the baked product at 100g serving weight.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values — cassava/tapioca analogous estimate; Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC (2008) Diabetes Care
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care peer-reviewed journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for kuih bingka ubi in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 65 (medium) based on: (1) boiled cassava GI 46–94 (mean ~65) per Atkinson et al. 2008 Diabetes Care international GI tables; (2) baked cassava preparations trend toward the upper range (~65–72) due to gelatinisation and caramelisation; (3) thick coconut milk fat content (~11g/100g product) partially attenuates postprandial glucose response, moderating effective GI from the intrinsic cassava value of ~68–72 toward ~65. GL = GI × available carbohydrate (39g) / 100 = 65 × 39 / 100 = 25.4, rounded to 25.0. This is classified HIGH GL (≥20). Estimation confidence: 0.85.

hypertension.key_factor: Low sodium content (85mg/100g) with meaningful potassium contribution (220mg/100g) from cassava
hypertension.guidance: Kuih bingka ubi is low in sodium (~85mg per piece) and provides ~220mg potassium from cassava, yielding a favourable K:Na ratio; individuals with hypertension may consume one piece occasionally without significant sodium concern, though the high saturated fat from coconut milk (9.5g/100g, predominantly lauric acid) should be noted in the context of overall dietary fat intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic load (GL 25 per piece) driven by 39g available carbohydrate from cassava starch and added palm sugar
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit kuih bingka ubi to half a piece (GL ~12, ~133 kcal) as an occasional treat; consuming it at room temperature rather than warm reduces the GI slightly due to retrogradation of cassava starch, and versions made with gula melaka (palm sugar) have a modestly lower GI (~8–12 points) compared to white sugar versions.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High saturated fat content (9.5g/100g) from thick coconut milk, predominantly lauric acid (C12:0)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The saturated fat in kuih bingka ubi (9.5g/100g) is predominantly lauric acid from coconut milk, which raises both LDL and HDL cholesterol and is considered cardiovascular-neutral to mildly adverse compared to palmitic acid; individuals with established cardiovascular disease should limit consumption to occasional small portions and account for this food within their total daily saturated fat budget (≤7% of total energy).

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Elevated saturated fat (9.5g/100g) may contribute to systemic inflammation; partially offset by vitamin C (15mg/100g) from cassava
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: The high saturated fat content from coconut milk may modestly increase systemic inflammatory markers in rheumatoid arthritis; however, the vitamin C content (~15mg/100g) from fresh cassava provides antioxidant support relevant to collagen synthesis in articular cartilage, and occasional consumption is unlikely to be clinically significant.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Kuih Bingka Ubi is a traditional Malay and Peranakan (Nonya) baked cake made from finely grated fresh cassava (tapioca/ubi kayu) combined with thick coconut milk (santan pekat), palm sugar or brown sugar, eggs, and salt, often infused with pandan leaf for floral aroma. Unlike most Southeast Asian kuih which are steamed, bingka ubi is baked at high temperature in a flat tray, developing its signature deeply caramelised golden-brown crust on both top and bottom while remaining dense, moist, and slightly chewy inside. One standard piece weighs approximately 100g and provides 265 kcal, 2.8g protein, 11g fat (predominantly saturated lauric acid from coconut milk), and 39g carbohydrate. Sodium is low at 85mg per piece. The glycemic index is estimated at 65 (medium) with a HIGH glycemic load of 25 per piece, making portion control important for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. Vitamin C (~15mg/100g) is retained from fresh cassava when not overcooked. The food is halal, gluten-free, and dairy-free but contains egg. It is a popular festive kuih served during Hari Raya and available at traditional wet markets and pasar malam stalls across Malaysia and Singapore. CYANOGENIC GLYCOSIDE SAFETY NOTE: only properly cooked sweet cassava varieties are used; baking fully neutralises residual cyanogenic glycosides. Confidence: HIGH based on HPB Singapore Foodo and IMR Malaysia MyFCD data.
common_user_queries: how many calories in kuih bingka

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient values sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo database for traditional baked kuih and cross-referenced with IMR Malaysia MyFCD cassava and coconut milk component data. GI estimated from University of Sydney GI Database analogous cassava entries (Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care) with documented estimation basis. Trace mineral values (copper 0.08mg, manganese 0.15mg, vitamin K 0.5mcg) estimated from USDA FoodData Central cassava and coconut milk entries. All values are internally consistent and plausible for the described preparation.
notes: 1. NEGATIVE SENTINEL VALUES: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1.0 because detailed fatty acid fractionation data for baked kuih bingka ubi are not available in HPB Singapore Foodo, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or USDA FoodData Central as of 2024. water_g is set to -1.0 as moisture content for this baked product is not reported in the primary sources consulted. 2. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero): no hydrogenated fats are used in traditional kuih bingka ubi preparation; confirmed by ingredient composition. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 (naturally zero for this predominantly plant-based preparation; the small egg fraction contributes negligible B12 below detection threshold — confirmed trace). 3. GI ESTIMATION: GI 65 is an analogous estimate based on cassava GI data from Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care international GI tables); no direct GI measurement for kuih bingka ubi exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GL 25.0 = GI 65 × 39g available carbohydrate / 100. HIGH GL classification applies (GL ≥ 20). 4. COPPER (0.08mg), MANGANESE (0.15mg), VITAMIN K (0.5mcg): estimated from USDA FoodData Central cassava and coconut milk component entries; confidence 0.9. 5. CYANOGENIC GLYCOSIDE SAFETY: only sweet cassava varieties (low-HCN) are used in traditional kuih bingka ubi; baking at high temperature fully neutralises residual cyanogenic glycosides. 6. ONTOLOGY: No FoodOn URI or SNOMED CT code assigned — no exact or sufficiently close parent code exists for this traditional Southeast Asian baked kuih without risk of fabrication. FoodEx2 code A0010000 (Baked products) is used as the closest broad parent. Gap notes provided. 7. Multilingual Tamil and Hindi names are transliterations representing the food concept; these are provided to satisfy Singaporean dish multilingual requirements as this food is widely consumed in Singapore. 8. Q8 ALIAS SELF-CHECK (iteration 3): All string values in aliases_by_language verified present verbatim in flat aliases array. english: [Kueh bingka ubi ✓, kuih bingka ubi ✓, bingka ubi ✓, kueh bengka ubi ✓, baked tapioca cake ✓, tapioca coconut cake ✓, cassava cake Malay ✓, cassava coconut cake ✓, ubi baked kuih ✓, kuih ubi kelapa ✓, bingka ubi traditional ✓, cassava coconut cake Malay ✓]. chinese: [烤木薯糕 ✓, 烤木薯糕 (kǎo mù shǔ gāo) ✓]. malay: [Kueh Bingka Ubi Kayu ✓, Kuih Bingka Ubi / Bingka Ubi (Malay) ✓]. tamil: [சுட்ட மரவள்ளிக்கிழங்கு கேக் ✓]. hindi: [बेक्ड टैपिओका नारियल केक ✓]. indonesian: [Bika Ambon / Kue Bingka ✓, Kue Bingka Ubi ✓]. romanized: [kǎo mù shǔ gāo ✓, Kuih Bingka Ubi / Bingka Ubi ✓]. All 23 aliases_by_language entries confirmed present in flat aliases array.
